coredata.dc:title: Elucidating the effects of −OH content on phase transition and Li-ion transport of anti-perovskite solid electrolytes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Xiao, Zunqiu
Wang, Huaying
Cai, Ningyuan
Li, Yutong
Xiang, Kejia
Wei, Wei
Ye, Tao
Zhang, Zhongtai
Wang, Shitong
Tang, Zilong
coredata.dc:description: Anti-perovskite materials such as Li2(OH)Cl have garnered considerable interest as solid electrolytes due to their numerous advantages. However, the low ionic conductivity of the orthorhombic Li2(OH)Cl near room temperature presents a significant challenge for the application. In this study, we intricately modulate the −OH content in Li2(OH)Cl through a controlled heat treatment process. This method effectively increases the cubic phase content and lowers the phase transition temperature, thereby enhancing the ionic conductivity at 30 °C by more than an order of magnitude. Theoretical calculations illustrate that the removal of −OH content significantly reduces the barrier for phase transition, leading to substantial alterations in the Li-ion transport pathway and migration barrier. Furthermore, LiHClO-600 demonstrates exceptional resistance to lithium reduction and is compatible with lithium metal and LiFePO4, rendering it a viable solid electrolyte for batteries. Both experimental findings and theoretical calculations cohesively highlight the pivotal role of −OH content in driving phase transition and facilitating Li-ion transport in anti-perovskite solid electrolytes, paving the way for their potential utilization in all-solid-state batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Anti-perovskite
Phase transition
−OH content
Solid electrolytes
Li-ion batteries

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of (a) orthorhombic phase, (b) cubic phase of Li2(OH)Cl. (c) XRD patterns, (d) XRD refinement of LiHClO-pristine and 600 samples, (e) change of weight proportion at various dehydration temperatures.

Variable-temperature XRD patterns of (a) LiHClO-pristine, and (b) LiHClO-600. (c) DSC results, (d) Raman spectra of LiHClO-T samples. Variable-temperature Raman spectra of (e) LiHClO-pristine, and (f) LiHClO-600.

Calculation of the migration paths before and after dehydration. (a) Schematic diagram of the migration path, (b) the energy barrier before dehydration. (c) Schematic diagram of the migration path, (d) the energy barrier after dehydration. Dark green is the migration path of Li+, and yellow is the removed −OH content in (a) and (c).
Kinetic studies of LiHClO-T samples. (a) Nyquist plots of LiHClO-600, (b) Arrhenius curves, (c) ion conductivities at 30 and 80 °C, and (d) DC polarization curves of LiHClO-600.
Calculations of Li+ ions migration in LiHClO-pristine and 600. (a-b) BVSE analysis, (c)MSD of various atoms for LiHClO-pristine. (d-e) BVSE analysis, (f)MSD of different atoms for LiHClO-600.

The electrochemical performance and cell performance of LiHClO-600. (a) CV curves, b) CCD test, and c) cycling test of Li|SE|Li cells. (d) Schematic diagram of LiFePO4|SE|Li cells, (e-f) cycling test of LiFePO4|LiHClO-600|Li cell.

head.ce:title: Elucidating the effects of −OH content on phase transition and Li-ion transport of anti-perovskite solid electrolytes

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Anti-perovskite materials such as Li2(OH)Cl have garnered considerable interest as solid electrolytes due to their numerous advantages. However, the low ionic conductivity of the orthorhombic Li2(OH)Cl near room temperature presents a significant challenge for the application. In this study, we intricately modulate the −OH content in Li2(OH)Cl through a controlled heat treatment process. This method effectively increases the cubic phase content and lowers the phase transition temperature, thereby enhancing the ionic conductivity at 30 °C by more than an order of magnitude. Theoretical calculations illustrate that the removal of −OH content significantly reduces the barrier for phase transition, leading to substantial alterations in the Li-ion transport pathway and migration barrier. Furthermore, LiHClO-600 demonstrates exceptional resistance to lithium reduction and is compatible with lithium metal and LiFePO4, rendering it a viable solid electrolyte for batteries. Both experimental findings and theoretical calculations cohesively highlight the pivotal role of −OH content in driving phase transition and facilitating Li-ion transport in anti-perovskite solid electrolytes, paving the way for their potential utilization in all-solid-state batteries.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: All-solid-state lithium batteries (ASSLBs), renowned for their high safety and energy density, are surfacing as a frontrunner for the forthcoming generation of battery technology. [1–3]. Solid electrolytes (SEs) are the core components of ASSLBs and determine the performance of ASSLBs [4–6].

Recent focus has shifted towards anti-perovskite materials such as Li2(OH)Cl as promising SEs, owing to their straightforward synthesis, low melting points, and compatibility with lithium metal [7–10]. However, the room-temperature orthorhombic phase of Li2(OH)Cl, with its relatively low ionic conductivity [11,12], poses a significant challenge for the further development of these materials in SE applications. A notable phase transition at 45 °C to a cubic phase results in an ionic conductivity increase by an order of magnitude [11,13], spurring a plethora of research aimed at stabilizing this cubic phase at room temperature and enhancing its ionic conductivity. Gao et al. successfully stabilized the cubic phase at room temperature by substituting K ions for [Li2OH] clusters, which resulted in solid-state batteries exhibiting exceptional cycling performance [14]. Li et al. found that replacing −OH with F in Li2(OH)Cl induced a transition from the orthorhombic to the cubic phase at room temperature, significantly boosting ionic conductivity [15]. In addition, it is posited that the high ionic conductivity in the cubic phase of Li2(OH)Cl is attributable to the 'paddlewheel' rotation of the −OH groups [16]. These studies underscore the critical role of −OH content in the stabilization of cubic phase and enhanced Li-ion transport. Nevertheless, the effects of −OH content on phase transition and Li-ion transport of anti-perovskite SEs near room temperature remain to be fully elucidated.

In this study, we strategically employed a controlled dehydration technique to precisely adjust the −OH content in Li2(OH)Cl. This process resulted in a notable shift towards the cubic phase and a corresponding decrease in the phase transition temperature. Through comprehensive computational analyses, we established that the removal of −OH content significantly contributes to lowering the phase transition barrier. The observed reduction in the diffusion barrier of the cubic phase, coupled with a marked increase in the mean square displacement (MSD) of Li atoms after dehydration, elucidates the substantial enhancement in ionic conductivity. LiHClO-600 demonstrates exceptional resistance to lithium reduction, making it an ideal candidate for direct integration with lithium metal and LiFePO4 in the fabrication of ASSLBs. This research elucidates the impact of −OH content on the phase transition in anti-perovskite structures, offering new insights into the utilization of anti-perovskite materials in ASSLBs.

Equal molar quantities of LiOH and LiCl (Aladdin, 99.9 %) were combined and heated to 400 °C, resulting in the formation of LiHClO-pristine. This compound was then subjected to thermal processing at varying temperatures to yield dehydrated variants, denoted as LiHClO-T, where T represents the specific temperature of heat treatment. All synthesis procedures were meticulously conducted inside a glove box (O2 < 1 ppm, H2O < 1 ppm) to inhibit any atmospheric interaction.

Structural analysis was conducted using X-ray diffraction (XRD) and variable-temperature XRD on a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer, utilizing Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.154 nm). The samples were carefully sealed in Kapton film to prevent air exposure during analysis. Rietveld refinement of the XRD data was performed using GSAS software. The thermo-gravimetric analysis combined with mass spectrometry (TG-MS) was employed to monitor thermal transitions during heating and to categorize the evolved products at elevated temperatures. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was used to record endothermic and exothermic events in the samples, heated at a rate of 5 °C min−1. Raman spectroscopy, along with variable-temperature Raman, was executed using a LabRAM Horiba spectrometer, with samples positioned in an air-insulated mold.

Custom molds were utilized to exclude air influence during the experiments. LiHClO-T samples were strategically positioned between stainless steel (ss) sheets to form a cell configuration termed ss|sample|ss, enabling the assessment of both lithium-ion and electronic conductivity. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed with a Zahner IM6 system across a frequency range of 1 Hz to 1 MHz, with an AC amplitude of 50 mV. Arrhenius plots were derived from Nyquist plots recorded within a temperature range of 30 ∼ 80 °C. Polarization curves at 0.8 V were also recorded for evaluating the electronic conductivity.

To assess the electrochemical stability, the sample was first melted onto a ss sheet. Fresh lithium foil was then layered on top, constructing a ss|SE|Li sandwich cell. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) of these cells was conducted using a CHI 660E instrument, within a voltage window of −0.5 ∼ 5.0 V (vs. Li+/Li) at a scan rate of 0.5 mV s−1. Lithium symmetric cells were fabricated by pressing 200 mg of LiHClO-T powder into pellets at 300 MPa pressure, sandwiched between fresh lithium foils. These Li|sample|Li cells underwent densification at 150 °C for 12 h and were then subjected to cycling at variable and constant current densities on Land C2001A battery systems at 120 °C.

The LiFePO4 electrode was prepared by mixing LiFePO4, carbon black, PVDF, and LiTFSI in a 3:3:2:2 wt ratio to form a slurry. The average LiFePO4 loading was maintained at about 0.30 mg cm−2. LiFePO4 disc was first placed, followed by an even distribution of 70 mg of LiHClO-T powder over the disc, which was then compressed at 300 MPa. Lithium foil was applied to the opposing side under 100 MPa pressure, culminating in the assembly of a complete LiFePO4|SE|Li cell. These assembled cells were then subjected to constant current charge–discharge testing within a voltage range of 2.7 ∼ 3.8 V (vs. Li+/Li) at 120 °C on LAND CT2001A battery systems.
The computational methods are demonstrated in the Supplementary information.

Li2(OH)Cl exhibits two distinct crystallographic structures: the orthorhombic phase (
Pban , N o . 50
) [17] and cubic phase (
Pm
3
¯
m , N o . 221
) [18], as depicted in Fig. 1
a-b. The cubic phase, characterized by its higher symmetry, contrasts markedly with the orthorhombic phase, which is defined by an asymmetric distribution of lithium ions. Specifically, in the cubic phase, lithium ions are uniformly distributed, occupying two-thirds of the available Li sites, with the remaining one-third being lithium vacancies, randomly interspersed without distinction. In the orthorhombic structure, however, there are two layers: one is filled with lithium ions, while the other maintains only one-second occupancy. This asymmetry is fundamental to the formation of the orthorhombic phase.

Here, LiHClO-pristine (Li2(OH)Cl) was synthesized by heating a mixture of LiOH and LiCl to 400 °C. Subsequent heat treatment of LiHClO-pristine at various temperatures resulted in products designated as LiHClO-T, where T denoted the heat treatment temperature. After heat treatment, the crystal structure exhibits a significant transition, as evidenced by the XRD patterns shown in Fig. 1c-e and Figure S1. TG-MS analysis (Figure S2) indicates that H2O is the primary component removed during the heat treatment process, suggesting that the varying −OH contents in LiHClO-T samples originate from the different heat treatment temperatures. XRD refinement of the LiHClO-pristine confirms its alignment with the orthorhombic phase (Fig. 1d), displaying no significant extraneous peaks. However, after heat treatment, a prominent shift to the cubic phase is observed. The most intense peaks near 33° evolve from three distinct orthorhombic peaks into a single, sharp peak, indicating the cubic phase and an enhanced symmetry. With increasing heat treatment temperatures, the proportion of the cubic phase in the samples rises while the orthorhombic phase diminishes (Fig. 1e). LiHClO-600 exhibits approximately equal proportions of both orthorhombic and cubic phases, indicating a partial phase transition to the cubic phase after the removal of −OH content. When the temperature rises to 700 °C, the emergence of LiCl and Li2O peaks signals the decomposition of LiHClO-pristine due to excessive removal of −OH content, underscoring the vital role of −OH content in ensuring the stability of the anti-perovskite structure.

Variable-temperature XRD, DSC, and Raman spectroscopy were employed to delve deeper into the influence of heat treatment on the phase transition of anti-perovskite materials. Illustrated in Fig. 2
a-b, the phase transition temperature of LiHClO-pristine is observed to be around 45 °C, which is in close agreement with existing literature [19]. Notably, upon removing −OH content, LiHClO-600 exhibits a phase transition at a reduced temperature of 40 °C. This downward shift in the phase transition temperature is further corroborated by DSC data presented in Fig. 2c. The DSC profile of LiHClO-pristine shows an endothermic peak at 43 °C, indicative of the orthorhombic to cubic phase transition. After heat treatment, a conspicuous shift of this peak to a lower temperature is evident, mirroring the trend of a decreasing phase transition temperature. Raman spectroscopic analysis of LiHClO-pristine reveals a prominent peak at 3600 cm−1. Following heat treatment, the emergence of a new peak at lower Raman shifts 3588 cm−1 is observed (Fig. 2d), signifying the formation of the cubic phase. Variable-temperature Raman spectroscopy further elucidates this phenomenon, showing a peak shift from 3600 to 3588 cm−1 at 45 °C in LiHClO-pristine (Fig. 2e), corresponding to the phase transition from orthorhombic to cubic phase. The transition temperature of LiHClO-600 is found to decrease to 40 °C after removal of −OH content (Fig. 2f).

The conducted experiments conclusively demonstrate that removal of −OH content after heat treatment leads to a reduction in the phase transition temperature, yet the underlying mechanism influencing this decrease remains elusive. A crucial aspect of the transition from the orthorhombic to the cubic phase involves the migration of lithium ions from a fully occupied layer to an adjacent layer, culminating in a random configuration. To decipher this process, the climbing image nudged elastic band (CI-NEB) method was applied to ascertain the energy barrier associated with the migration of lithium ions from the fully occupied layer to a sparsely occupied layer, both before and after the removal of −OH content. This can serve as a simple indicator of the ease of phase transition. The results reveal a notable decrease in the migration energy barrier following dehydration, with values diminishing from 0.498 eV to 0.384 eV (Fig. 3 ). This significant reduction elucidates that the removal of −OH content effectively lowers the energy barrier, thereby facilitating the phase transition process. Consequently, this mechanistic insight into the reduced energy barrier provides a reasonable explanation for the observed decrease in the temperature threshold required for the phase transition after heat treatment.

The ionic conductivity, both before and after dehydration, was meticulously evaluated using EIS. The resulting Nyquist plots, as illustrated in Fig. 4
a, exhibit characteristic high-frequency semicircles coupled with low-frequency linear traits. Fig. 4b delineates the Arrhenius plots for the samples across a temperature range of 30 ∼ 80 °C. A pronounced escalation in ionic conductivity is observed at 43 °C for Li2(OH)Cl, aligning with the phase transition from orthorhombic to cubic. As reported by Song et al., Li2OHCl does not undergo a complete transition to the cubic phase at 40 °C, potentially maintaining an intermediate orthorhombic structure [20]. LiHClO-pristine undergoes a full transition to the cubic phase, which is reflected in a marked inflection point in ionic conductivity at 43 °C, escalating from 2.51 × 10−8 S cm−1 at 30 °C to 5.77 × 10−6 S cm−1 at 43 °C. In contrast, LiHClO-600 manifests a phase transition temperature at 38 °C. Notably, the ionic conductivity at 30 °C of LiHClO-600 exhibits a significant enhancement, increasing by an order of magnitude from 2.51 × 10−8 S cm−1 to 4.31 × 10−7 S cm−1 (Fig. 4c), attributed to the presence of the cubic phase. The electronic conductivity of LiHClO-600 is approximately 10−10 S cm−1 (Fig. 4d), indicating that LiHClO-600 is an excellent ionic conductor.

To shed light on the pronounced differences in ionic conductivity observed at low temperatures before and after the removal of −OH content, the bond-valence site energy (BVSE) method was applied to analyze the migration pathways and energy barriers associated with Li+ diffusion. Illustrated in Fig. 5
a-b, the orthorhombic phase exhibits a notable diffusion barrier along the Li1−Li2 trajectory, measured at 0.620 eV. This value is considerably higher than the barriers observed for intra-layer diffusion, such as between Li2 and Li3, making cross-layer diffusion particularly arduous in the orthorhombic structure. In contrast, as demonstrated in Fig. 5d-e, the cubic phase, after dehydration, shows a random arrangement of lithium sites. This randomness equates the diffusion barriers across and within layers to 0.339 eV, a significant reduction compared to the 0.620 eV cross-layer and 0.404 eV intra-layer barriers in the orthorhombic phase.

To further dissect the observed variance in ionic conductivity, Ab initio molecular dynamics (AIMD) simulations were employed. These simulations delved into the diffusion dynamics of various atoms within the materials. In the orthorhombic phase, the MSD of Li atoms keeps steady as the simulation progresses (Fig. 5c), mirroring the extremely low ionic conductivity characteristic of this phase. Conversely, in the dehydrated cubic phase, Li atoms exhibit marked diffusion behavior and multi-directional migration pathways. The MSD of Li atoms was significantly greater than that of other ions (Fig. 5f), underscoring the dominance of Li+ diffusion in the cubic phase. This observation is in harmony with the measured ionic conductivity, which is predominantly attributed to lithium ions, aligning with existing literature [19] that delineates the long-range diffusion of lithium ions and the short-range diffusion of hydrogen atoms. Both experimental and computational results highlight the critical influence of −OH content and crystal structure symmetry on lithium ion diffusion within anti-perovskite materials.

The electrochemical stability and battery performance of LiHClO-600 were evaluated. As shown in Fig. 6
a, CV analysis of the Li|SE|ss cell indicated that the initial current response for LiHClO-600 was markedly higher than that in subsequent cycles. An interface layer between the SE and the lithium metal was formed and composed of a mixture of Li2O and LiCl (Figure S4), which is consistent with the literatures [21,22]. By the fourth and fifth cycles, a convergence of the CV curves was observed, indicative of a stabilized interface layer. Analysis of the data from the fifth cycle revealed an oxidation potential for LiHClO-600 at around 4.0 V, making it well-suited for use with cathodes like LiFePO4, which have charging voltages below 4.0 V. Lithium symmetric cells were employed to assess the resistance of LiHClO-600 to lithium reduction. As shown in Fig. 6b-c, the cell demonstrated the ability to stably cycle for 200cycles at a current density of 0.10 mA cm−2, with a critical current density (CCD) of 0.35 mA cm−2. This performance highlights its excellent resistance to lithium reduction. Notably, overpotentials in the initial cycles showed a decreasing trend, aligning with phenomena related to interface activation reported in prior studies [23,24]. To further explore the applicability of LiHClO-600 as a SE, an all-solid-state cell comprising LiFePO4|SE|Li was assembled and tested under pressure, as illustrated in Fig. 6d. This cell exhibited a capacity of 125.3 mAh/g at a 1.0 C rate (Fig. 6e-f). Significantly, this fabrication process did not involve the introduction of any liquid electrolytes or interface modification layers at any stage. The interface between LiHClO-600 and LiFePO4 deteriorates significantly (Figure S5), which is the main reason for the degradation of specific capacity.

In summary, increasing heat treatment temperature would reduce −OH content in Li2(OH)Cl, resulting in the enhancement of cubic phase and the reduction of phase transition temperature. Consequently, the ionic conductivity of LiHClO-600, after dehydration, is substantially improved, surpassing that of LiHClO-pristine at 30 °C by more than an order of magnitude. Computational analysis further reveals that the reduction in −OH content significantly lowers the energy barrier for the phase transition, contributing to the observed decrease in the phase transition temperature. In the cubic phase, a lower diffusion barrier is noted compared to the orthorhombic phase, and the markedly higher MSD of Li atoms in the cubic phase corroborates its superior ionic conductivity. LiHClO-600 exhibits excellent lithium reduction resistance, with a critical current density of 0.35 mA cm−2 and stable cycling over 200cycles at 0.10 mA cm−2. Furthermore, LiHClO-600 demonstrates compatibility with LiFePO4, achieving 125.3 mAh/g capacity at a 1.0 C rate without requiring liquid electrolyte or interface modification. These findings underscore the importance of −OH content in the phase transition of anti-perovskite structures, offering new perspectives for their application in ASSLBs.

Zunqiu Xiao: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Huaying Wang: Writing – review & editing, Software, Conceptualization. Ningyuan Cai: Methodology. Yutong Li: Writing – review & editing. Kejia Xiang: Investigation. Wei Wei: Methodology. Tao Ye: Conceptualization. Zhongtai Zhang: Supervision. Shitong Wang: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Zilong Tang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Zilong Tang acknowledges support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52172210 and No. 51772163).
Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.elecom.2024.107684.

The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
